text: The surgeon inserts a calibrating bougie into the stomach and identifies the stomach. Short gastric vessels connecting the stomach to the spleen are exposed and divided using a robotic vessel sealer, employing traction and counter traction techniques. This prepares the area for the upcoming fundoplication.

The surgeon uses the robotic vessel sealer to apply gentle pressure on the stomach, creating a window near the spleen. The vessel sealer effectively identifies, seals, and transects vessels as the operation approaches the hiatus or gastroesophageal junction, where the stomach connects to the esophagus. This area is freed to allow for later identification and re-approximation of the diaphragm muscles.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper in the right hand to separate the esophagus from the left crux of the diaphragm, applying pressure in the opposite direction for traction and counter traction. The procedure involves four small 8mm incisions. The vessel sealer is used to open the pars flaccida, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) beneath the liver. The surgeon carefully separates the esophagus from the right crux of the diaphragm, identifying an avascular plane while preserving the vagus nerve. The dissection connects from the patient's left side to the right side.

The surgeon identifies the crux of the diaphragm and enters the thoracic cavity, carefully dissecting around small perforators and vessels. The esophagus is freed and mobilized, ensuring its connection with the stomach remains within the abdominal cavity. Adhesions are released to prevent the hernia from retracting.

The surgeon identifies the left and right crura of the diaphragm and frees the connections between the esophagus and diaphragm. An Ethabon suture is placed laparoscopically to prepare for closing the diaphragmatic hernia. The surgeon uses a robotic system to facilitate visualization and tissue manipulation, employing a simple slip knot technique to approximate the muscles without strangulation. The vessel sealer is used to cut the suture after placement.

The surgeon cinches a slip knot to secure the repair, ensuring the sizing tube in the patient's esophagus is not constricted to avoid discomfort or dysphagia. The hiatal hernia is closed to contribute to the anti-reflux mechanism. The surgeon prepares a window for the Nissen fundoplication, removing adhesions to prevent ischemia and ensure a clear area for the wrap.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the fundus of the stomach, preparing for a 360-degree Nissen fundoplication wrap. The stomach is pulled through, ensuring the wrap is loose and floppy to avoid ischemia or stenosis. The surgeon performs a 'shoe shine' maneuver to confirm the wrap's mobility. Sutures are placed: the first high on the fundus, the second stabilizing through the esophagus, and the third on the right side of the stomach. The vessel sealer is used to cut the sutures, ensuring they are snug but not too tight.

The surgeon uses a robotic system to perform suturing for the Nissen fundoplication, taking alternating throws to recreate the sphincter mechanism at the top of the stomach wrap. The needle driver is used to snug down the sutures, and the vessel sealer cuts and removes extra suture material. The surgeon incorporates a healthy portion of the esophagus to prevent slippage of the hiatal hernia, utilizing the three-dimensional view provided by the robotic optics.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree scope to inspect the newly created Nissen fundoplication, ensuring the wrap is loose. A gastropaxi suture is placed at the top of the Nissen through the right crus of the diaphragm to prevent slippage. The vessel sealer is used to manage the suture, and the assistant removes it. An injection of ICG dye is performed, and the Firefly technique is used to check for ischemia. An intraoperative endoscopy is conducted to evaluate the esophagus and the wrap, ensuring the wrap is not too tight and the stomach's perfusion is adequate.

The surgeon uses an eight-millimeter port to flip up and inspect the fundoplication wrap from below, ensuring proper placement and integrity. The assistant closes the skin incisions, particularly focusing on those larger than twelve millimeters, while the surgeon removes all residual gas from the patient.
